nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving and still forest streams, backwaters, and flooded areas in the Peruvian Amazon basin; shallow, heavily vegetated leaf-litter zones with soft, tannin-stained water over sandy or silty substrates

decorPreferences: fine sandy substrate (for digging and cave placement)
caves and coconut shells as spawning sites
dense planting (Amazon swords, cryptocorynes, floating plants for subdued lighting)
broken line-of-sight with rocks or wood to reduce male harassment of females

diet.notes: Insectivore in the wild, feeding on small invertebrates, insect larvae, worms, and microcrustaceans. In captivity, readily accepts high-quality frozen or live foods — daphnia, artemia (brine shrimp), bloodworms (Chironomus larvae), micro-worms, and white worms. Dry micro-pellets and small cichlid-specific granules are accepted by acclimatised specimens but live and frozen foods are strongly preferred and essential for conditioning before breeding.

goodWith: small peaceful tetras (cardinal tetras, rummy-nose tetras)
small corydoras catfish (upper layer of tank — avoid bottom-dwellers near spawning sites)
avoidWith: other Apistogramma species (interspecific aggression, hybridisation risk)
bottom-dwelling fish that compete for cave territories (loaches, other substrate-dwellers)
very small nano fish or shrimp when a breeding pair is guarding fry

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase photo gallery for Apistogramma eunotus (species ID 13217). Verify individual image licenses on the detail pages; many FishBase photos are contributed under permissive terms.
Wikimedia Commons category page for Apistogramma eunotus. Check for CC-licensed images of locality forms ('Tigre', 'Pebas').

summary: A robust Peruvian Amazon dwarf cichlid from the regani group, notable for its broader pH tolerance (6.0–7.5) compared to blackwater specialists. Males develop vivid yellow-and-blue breeding coloration; females turn bright yellow when guarding eggs in caves. Multiple locality forms ('Tigre', 'Pebas') exist, each with subtle color differences.
careNotes: Apistogramma eunotus is a member of the regani species group and originates from the Peruvian Amazon, with localities including the Río Tigre and Río Pebas drainage systems. Different locality populations ('Tigre form', 'Pebas form') show subtle differences in male coloration and should not be mixed to preserve locality integrity. Unlike many Apistogramma species that demand strict blackwater conditions, A. eunotus is notably water-parameter tolerant — it performs well across pH 6.0–7.5, making it accessible to hobbyists without RO water or heavy tannin supplementation, though soft water (gH 1–10) and moderate acidity are still preferred. The species is a cave-spawner that lives in harems in the wild; a typical aquarium setup pairs one male with two to three females, each female holding a distinct cave territory. Males are polygynous and will patrol all female territories. A species tank or a Peruvian biotope setup with compatible small tetras is ideal. Leaf litter (Indian almond/catappa leaves) is highly beneficial — it lowers pH, releases tannins, and provides shelter and microorganism growth for fry to feed on. Females become extremely territorial and aggressive toward other tank inhabitants (including the male) when brooding. The male should be removed or provided with escape routes via visual barriers once spawning begins if the tank is small. Weekly partial water changes of 25–30% help maintain water quality without disrupting the soft-water chemistry.
breedingNotes: Haremic cave-spawner; females select and vigorously defend a small cave (coconut shell, clay pot, flat rock overhang). A typical brood consists of 40–100 reddish eggs, which the female deposits on the cave ceiling and guards fiercely. Incubation takes approximately 48–72 hours at 26°C; fry become free-swimming after a further 5–7 days. The female provides exclusive parental care — she guards, fans, and herds the fry while aggressively driving away the male and any other fish. Fry accept newly hatched artemia nauplii and microworms as first foods; paramecia or infusoria can be offered in the first few days before the fry are large enough for nauplii. Conditioning both sexes on live and frozen foods prior to breeding significantly improves spawning success and clutch size. Water temperature at the higher end of the range (26–28°C) encourages spawning. Males may produce multiple clutches with multiple females simultaneously in a harem setup.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Apistogramma eunotus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided native range (Peruvian Amazon, Río Tigre, Río Pebas localities), water parameters (temperature 23–29°C, pH 6.0–7.5, hardness 18–143 ppm / ~1–8 dH), adult size (males to 8 cm, females to 4 cm), diet (carnivore/insectivore), regani-group taxonomy, haremic cave-spawning behavior, and care/breeding notes.

additional.name: FishBase — Apistogramma eunotus
additional.notes: Cross-referenced taxonomy (Cichlidae, Cichliformes), adult size (males to 8 cm TL), native range (Peru: Ucayali, Marañón, and Tigre river drainages), temperature range, and diet (invertivore/carnivore). Confirmed Kullander 1986 original description.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Apistogramma Care Guide
additional.notes: General Apistogramma care guide covering tank setup, diet (frozen/live insect larvae, small crustaceans), harem grouping, cave requirements, and water parameter guidance (pH 6.0–7.0 preferred, temperature 76–84°F / 24–29°C). Used to cross-check hobbyist-oriented care data and minimum tank size recommendations.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Apistogramma species guide
additional.notes: Background on Apistogramma species groups including regani group membership, locality form significance, and South American biotope setup recommendations. Confirmed haremic breeding strategy and female-only parental care characteristic of the genus.

additional.name: Cichlid Room Companion — Apistogramma eunotus
additional.notes: Specialist cichlid database. Provided additional locality data (Tigre and Pebas forms), notes on male/female size dimorphism, color descriptions, and breeding behavior cross-reference. Confirmed regani-group placement per Kullander's classification.

fish.breedingNotes: Haremic cave-spawner; female provides exclusive parental care of 40–100 eggs. Condition with live/frozen foods; provide caves and leaf litter. Fry accept artemia nauplii and microworms when free-swimming (~7–10 days post-spawn). Multiple females can spawn simultaneously in a harem setup.
